title: What Should a Good App Design Include?
excerpt: Great app design goes far beyond making screens look pretty. A truly effective mobile app design includes thoughtful UX architecture, smooth onboarding, intuitive navigation, and the right technical foundations. This checklist covers everything your app design should have.
content:
When building a mobile app, the quality of design determines whether users stay or uninstall. Here's what a professional, comprehensive app design should deliver.

1. Clear User Flows and Architecture

Before any visual design happens, the structure of the app must be mapped out. Good app design starts with information architecture — knowing exactly what screens exist, how they connect, and how a user moves through the app to accomplish their goals.

2. Thoughtful Onboarding

First impressions are permanent. A great onboarding flow welcomes new users, explains the app's value, collects only the information it absolutely needs, and gets users to the core experience as fast as possible.

3. Intuitive Navigation

Users should never wonder "how do I get to X." Navigation patterns — bottom tabs, side drawers, stack navigation — should feel native to the platform and consistent throughout the app.

4. Responsive, Scalable UI Components

A well-designed app has a design system: consistent buttons, input fields, cards, typography, and color usage across all screens. This ensures visual cohesion and makes development faster.

5. Accessibility Compliance

Good app design is inclusive. This means sufficient color contrast, large enough touch targets (minimum 44x44 points), support for screen readers, and dynamic text sizing.

6. Empty and Error States

Many apps neglect edge cases. What does the screen look like when there's no data? When a network error occurs? When a form is filled out incorrectly? Designing these states is a mark of professionalism.

7. Interactive Prototype

Before handing off to developers, a good design process produces an interactive prototype that can be user-tested — reducing the risk of expensive rework after development begins.
